---
title: Migrating from R blogdown to Quarto blogging
author: Jeffrey S. Racine
date: 20123-08-11
draft: false
---

Ahh, the joys and sorrows of technological progress! 

Some background is likely in order (and an apology for neglecting my blog). In 2018 I discovered R blogdown which was a big advance (for me at least) in creating a blog. The use of RStudio/blogdown for building websites was a very straightforward matter, and some features that streamlined creating new posts are in fact missing from Quarto's blog (at the moment, at least). However, this setup came with a set of issues, and having to actively maintain a theme (the hugo theme) and modify the theme to accomplish certain tasks created conditions for that blog incarnation's eventual neglect. In addition, I was using the free hosting platform netlify for hosting the site, and leveraging its ability to pull updated code from a GitHub repo and automatically rebuild the blog. But after not too much time had elapsed since adopting this approach, it became unmaintainable (read "not worth the effort" - netlify issuing security warnings about the hugo theme, issues with GitHub's security features and moving away from passwords, etc., conspired against this framework). In short, I was unwilling to invest the time to debug and keep this framework current, hence it lapsed which explains the large gap between some posts.

But with my recent experiences using Quarto being quite positive, and with Quarto^[Quarto is a multi-language, next-generation version of R Markdown from Posit and includes dozens of new features and capabilities while at the same being able to render most existing Rmd files without modification.] broadening its embrace beyond RStudio and R (and re-branding as Posit), it is clear that clinging to R blogdown is a recipe for pain that I don't need.

So, while visiting a co-author at the University of Pretoria this month, and after the jet lag had passed, I decided to dedicate part of the morning to migrating my old website to the Quarto blog framework and to host it directly on GitHub Pages (I am a big fan of GitHub Pages as you can see).

The migration was very straightforward and in less than one hour I was up and running with the migrated site (probably closer to 1/2 an hour of work at most). So far I am very happy with the result, and there is far less to maintain than when using R blogdown (at least this is my recollection). And hopefully this platform turns out to be more "future-proofed" than its predecessor. I am more than happy to discuss details with you if you wish (shoot me an email!). Time will tell if this is more future-proofed than its predecessor.
